svg.title: How Inky Bird Frame works
svg.desc: Runtime architecture. Internet services sit at the top: observation services (iNaturalist, eBird, BirdWeather), licensed reference and research sources, and OpenAI services for Codex generation and review. The controller initiates HTTPS (TCP 443) to those services. On the trusted private network, the Raspberry Pi display node initiates HTTP GET requests to the controller's configured port (8793 in the example configuration) and the controller returns approved catalog metadata and PNG plate images. The display node verifies checksums, applies the rotation mode, and drives the Inky Impression panel over GPIO/SPI. A closing note warns that the controller port is unauthenticated and must stay off the public internet; optional SSH (TCP 22) reaches the display from a setup computer.
svg.text: Codex CLI runs on the controller
Discovers, creates, reviews, and serves approved plates
— any of these can host it
Wi-Fi, Ethernet, routed private network, or VPN
Controller needs a stable name or address; the display can use ordinary DHCP
Downloads, verifies, selects, and shows approved plates
recommended — any supported 40-pin Raspberry Pi works
The service has no built-in authentication or TLS, so never expose its configured port to
the internet. Across untrusted networks, use a VPN or an authenticated TLS reverse
Optional display administration: SSH (TCP 22) from your setup computer
